content.text: A GenAI portfolio without segmentation is a flat list of requests that competes for the same budget, the same platform capacity, and the same governance attention — regardless of how different the use cases actually are. Segmenting the portfolio into tiers with different governance, funding, and delivery models lets the CoE apply the right level of scrutiny and support to each use case, rather than treating a document summarisation tool and a credit decision system with identical overhead.

attrs.alt: GenAI use-case portfolio matrix: value vs implementation risk

content.text: Why Segmentation Matters

The two variables that most determine what a use case needs from the CoE are business value and implementation risk. A high-value, low-risk use case should be fast-tracked with minimal friction. A high-value, high-risk use case needs a structured approval process, a monitoring plan, and an incident response runbook. A low-value, high-risk use case should almost never be funded.

Without explicit segmentation, one of two failure modes emerges: the CoE applies maximum scrutiny to everything (fast use cases get delayed; BUs resent the overhead) or it applies minimal scrutiny to everything (high-risk use cases go live without adequate governance; an incident follows).

Segmentation is the mechanism by which the CoE says 'we know these use cases are different, and we govern them differently.' It is not about creating bureaucracy — it is about matching oversight to actual risk.
The Two-Axis Scoring Model
Each use case submitted to the portfolio is scored on two axes. These scores determine its portfolio tier and the governance path it follows:
Axis 1: Business value
Score
Value Level
Criteria
3 — High
Strategic or significant financial impact
Documented ROI > $500K/yr, or enables a new product/market, or reduces a critical operational risk
2 — Medium
Meaningful but bounded impact
Documented ROI $100K–$500K/yr, or meaningfully reduces headcount requirement in a specific process
1 — Low
Marginal or convenience impact
Saves time but impact < $100K/yr; no strategic differentiation; nice-to-have
Axis 2: Implementation risk
Score
Risk Level
Criteria
1 — Low
Internal only, non-consequential
No PII/PHI; outputs are not used in decisions affecting people; failure mode is inconvenience (wrong summary), not harm
2 — Medium
Customer-facing OR processes sensitive data OR informs decisions
Exposes outputs to customers; processes PII/PHI; or outputs inform (but are reviewed by humans before) consequential decisions
3 — High
Fully automated consequential decisions OR regulated output domain
AI output triggers automated action with no human review (credit denial, medication dosing, employment decision); or output is a regulated artefact (financial advice, legal opinion)

# Use-case scoring model
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class ValueScore(Enum):
    LOW    = 1
    MEDIUM = 2
    HIGH   = 3

class RiskScore(Enum):
    LOW    = 1
    MEDIUM = 2
    HIGH   = 3

class PortfolioTier(Enum):
    TIER_1_QUICK_WIN     = "Tier 1: Quick Win"
    TIER_2_STRATEGIC_BET = "Tier 2: Strategic Bet"
    TIER_3_FILL_IN       = "Tier 3: Fill-In"
    TIER_4_REJECT        = "Tier 4: Reject / Defer"

@dataclass
class UseCase:
    id: str
    title: str
    bu: str
    value: ValueScore
    risk: RiskScore
    notes: str = ""

    @property
    def tier(self) -> PortfolioTier:
        v, r = self.value.value, self.risk.value

        # High value, low-medium risk → Quick Win
        if v == 3 and r <= 2:
            return PortfolioTier.TIER_1_QUICK_WIN
        if v == 2 and r == 1:
            return PortfolioTier.TIER_1_QUICK_WIN

        # High value, high risk OR medium value + medium risk → Strategic Bet
        if v == 3 and r == 3:
            return PortfolioTier.TIER_2_STRATEGIC_BET
        if v == 2 and r == 2:
            return PortfolioTier.TIER_2_STRATEGIC_BET
        if v == 2 and r == 3:
            return PortfolioTier.TIER_2_STRATEGIC_BET

        # Low value, low-medium risk → Fill-In
        if v == 1 and r <= 2:
            return PortfolioTier.TIER_3_FILL_IN

        # Low value, high risk → Reject
        return PortfolioTier.TIER_4_REJECT

    def portfolio_summary(self) -> str:
        return f"[{self.tier.value}] {self.title} (V={self.value.name}, R={self.risk.name})"

# Example portfolio scoring
use_cases = [
    UseCase("uc-001", "Legal Contract Summariser",     "Legal",   ValueScore.HIGH,   RiskScore.LOW),
    UseCase("uc-002", "Customer Support Bot",           "Service", ValueScore.HIGH,   RiskScore.MEDIUM),
    UseCase("uc-003", "Credit Decisioning Assistant",   "Finance", ValueScore.HIGH,   RiskScore.HIGH),
    UseCase("uc-004", "Internal HR FAQ Bot",            "HR",      ValueScore.LOW,    RiskScore.LOW),
    UseCase("uc-005", "Automated Audit Report",         "Audit",   ValueScore.LOW,    RiskScore.HIGH),
    UseCase("uc-006", "Code Review Assistant",          "Eng",     ValueScore.MEDIUM, RiskScore.LOW),
    UseCase("uc-007", "Finance Report Drafting",        "Finance", ValueScore.HIGH,   RiskScore.LOW),
    UseCase("uc-008", "Drug Interaction Checker",       "MedAff",  ValueScore.HIGH,   RiskScore.HIGH),
]

for uc in use_cases:
    print(uc.portfolio_summary())
# [Tier 1: Quick Win] Legal Contract Summariser (V=HIGH, R=LOW)
# [Tier 2: Strategic Bet] Customer Support Bot (V=HIGH, R=MEDIUM)
# [Tier 2: Strategic Bet] Credit Decisioning Assistant (V=HIGH, R=HIGH)
# [Tier 3: Fill-In] Internal HR FAQ Bot (V=LOW, R=LOW)
# [Tier 4: Reject / Defer] Automated Audit Report (V=LOW, R=HIGH)
# [Tier 1: Quick Win] Code Review Assistant (V=MEDIUM, R=LOW)
# [Tier 1: Quick Win] Finance Report Drafting (V=HIGH, R=LOW)
# [Tier 2: Strategic Bet] Drug Interaction Checker (V=HIGH, R=HIGH)
Portfolio Tiers: Governance and Delivery Model

Each tier gets a different governance path, funding model, and delivery support level. The goal is to minimise overhead for low-risk, high-value use cases while maintaining rigorous controls for high-risk ones.
Tier 1: Quick Wins
Quick wins are the backbone of programme momentum. They should be in production within 3 months of intake. The CoE's role is to enable, not gate.
Element
Quick Win Model
Intake SLA
5 business days from submission to provisioning decision
Approval authority
CoE Lead alone (no steering committee review required)
Platform setup
Self-serve for Tier 1 use cases once self-serve intake is live (Phase 3); CoE-assisted in Phase 2
Guardrails required
Standard guardrail package: PII detection, toxicity filter, output logging
Monitoring
Standard platform monitoring (latency, error rate, token usage); no custom monitoring required
Time to production target
< 8 weeks from intake approval to first production traffic
Escalation trigger
Any incident where AI output caused user harm, data exposure, or regulatory enquiry — escalates immediately to Tier 2 process
Tier 2: Strategic Bets

Strategic bets require structured governance because their failure modes are significant. The CoE's role shifts from enabler to co-designer — the CoE and BU jointly own the design, the risk mitigation, and the post-live monitoring plan.
Element
Strategic Bet Model
Intake SLA
15 business days (includes risk assessment, legal/compliance review, steering committee slot)
Approval authority
Steering committee (BU sponsor + CoE Lead + Legal/Compliance + CISO)
Platform setup
CoE-led; custom guardrails configured per use case; integration test plan required
Guardrails required
Custom guardrail stack: use-case-specific output filters, human-review checkpoint in workflow, adversarial test suite
Monitoring
Custom dashboard: hallucination rate proxy, user override rate, escalation rate, confidence distribution
Time to production target
8–16 weeks from intake approval; includes risk validation sprint before production
Escalation trigger
Any P1 incident; any regulatory enquiry; user override rate > 15%; hallucination rate proxy > 5%
Tier 3: Fill-Ins

Fill-ins are funded only when the CoE team has delivery capacity after Tier 1 and 2 work is on track. They are a useful source of team training exercises and internal tooling, but should never delay Tier 1 or 2 deliverables.
Element
Fill-In Model
Intake SLA
Backlog — no committed SLA; reviewed quarterly for promotion to Tier 1 or deprioritisation
Approval authority
CoE Lead; no steering committee slot required
Platform setup
BU-led with CoE documentation; minimal CoE engineering involvement
Guardrails required
Standard guardrail package
Monitoring
Standard platform monitoring only
Funding
BU-funded (CoE does not allocate headcount to Tier 3 delivery)
Tier 4: Reject / Defer

Tier 4 use cases are not funded. The CoE issue a rejection with a written rationale that explains which risk criteria the use case fails and what would need to change for it to be reconsidered. This is important: rejection is not permanent, and a clear written rationale gives the BU a pathway to re-submit if they can address the concern.

# Tier 4 rejection notice generator
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RejectionNotice:
    use_case_id: str
    title: str
    risk_issues: list[str]
    reconsideration_criteria: list[str]
    reviewer: str

    def render(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"PORTFOLIO REVIEW OUTCOME: {self.use_case_id} — {self.title}",
            f"Decision: REJECTED (Tier 4 — Low value, High risk)",
            f"Reviewed by: {self.reviewer}",
            "",
            "Risk issues identified:",
        ]
        for issue in self.risk_issues:
            lines.append(f"  • {issue}")
        lines.append("")
        lines.append("Criteria for reconsideration:")
        for criterion in self.reconsideration_criteria:
            lines.append(f"  • {criterion}")
        lines.append("")
        lines.append("This decision may be appealed to the steering committee within 30 days.")
        return "\n".join(lines)

notice = RejectionNotice(
    use_case_id="uc-005",
    title="Automated Audit Report",
    risk_issues=[
        "Output constitutes a regulated audit opinion — requires licensed auditor sign-off",
        "No human review checkpoint in proposed workflow (fully automated output delivery)",
        "Proposed value ($60K/yr) does not justify governance overhead for a Tier 3 risk profile",
    ],
    reconsideration_criteria=[
        "Scope reduced to draft preparation only (auditor reviews and approves before delivery)",
        "Human-in-the-loop checkpoint documented in workflow design",
        "Value case updated to reflect draft-only scope",
    ],
    reviewer="CoE Lead",
)
print(notice.render())
Portfolio Composition Targets
The steering committee should review the portfolio composition quarterly. An unhealthy portfolio is one that is skewed too heavily in any one direction:
Portfolio Composition Signal
What It Indicates
Corrective Action
70%+ of active use cases are Tier 1
Healthy for Phase 2; in Phase 3 suggests BUs are not bringing genuinely transformational use cases to the CoE
Run a use-case ideation workshop with each BU to identify Tier 2 candidates from their strategy
50%+ of active use cases are Tier 2
Programme may be understaffed for the governance overhead; Tier 2 use cases take 3× the CoE time of Tier 1
Either grow CoE capacity or deliberately throttle Tier 2 intake to match capacity
Tier 3 items outnumber Tier 1 in the queue
BUs are submitting low-value requests; programme may lack clear value criteria communicated to BUs
Publish the scoring rubric with examples; require BU sponsor sign-off that value case meets Tier 1 threshold before submission
Zero Tier 4 rejections
Either portfolio is genuinely clean, or use cases are not being scrutinised
Review recent intake decisions for any use cases that were approved without adequate risk documentation
Evolving Tier Assignments
Tier assignments are not permanent. Use cases move between tiers as three factors change:
Technology maturity

— A use case that was Tier 2 because guardrail technology was immature may be re-evaluated as Tier 1 once proven guardrails are available on the platform. The customer support bot that required a Tier 2 governance process in 2024 may warrant Tier 1 review in 2026 when the CoE's safety stack is battle-tested.
Regulatory environment

— EU AI Act Article 6 classifications may shift a use case's risk profile regardless of the underlying technology. A use case that was Tier 1 (low risk) before a new regulation may be reclassified to Tier 2 or higher when the regulation takes effect.
Demonstrated track record

— A BU that has run three Tier 1 use cases successfully and has established a trained AI guild may have lower delivery risk than a BU with no track record — the same use case might be Tier 1 for the experienced BU and Tier 2 for the new one.

The CoE should review tier assignments for active use cases every 6 months and whenever a significant regulatory or technology change occurs. Promotions (Tier 2 → Tier 1) reduce governance overhead as the programme matures; demotions (Tier 1 → Tier 2) are triggered by incidents or changed risk profiles.
Communicating the Scoring Model to BUs
The scoring model is only useful if BUs understand it well enough to self-classify their submissions before submitting. The CoE should publish:
The scoring rubric with worked examples (publish at least 3 real scored examples from pilot use cases)
A self-assessment checklist BUs complete at intake submission time
The governance path for each tier so BUs know what to expect before they submit
Clear escalation path if a BU disagrees with the tier assignment (steering committee review within 30 days)

If BUs consistently misclassify their use cases at intake, the scoring model is too complex or too opaque. Simplify the rubric until a BU product manager can correctly classify 80% of use cases on their first attempt without CoE assistance.
Summary
Segment the portfolio on two axes — business value (Low/Medium/High) and implementation risk (Low/Medium/High) — to produce four tiers: Quick Win, Strategic Bet, Fill-In, Reject

Each tier gets a different governance path: Tier 1 is fast-track (5-day SLA, CoE Lead approval); Tier 2 is structured (15-day SLA, steering committee); Tier 3 is backlog with BU-funded delivery; Tier 4 is rejected with written rationale
Portfolio composition targets: Tier 1 should dominate (40–60%); Tier 2 is bounded by CoE governance capacity; Tier 3 is opportunistic; Tier 4 should be non-zero (indicates genuine scrutiny)
Tier assignments evolve: technology maturity, regulatory changes, and BU track record all affect the appropriate tier for a given use case type
Publish the scoring model with worked examples; if BUs cannot self-classify correctly, simplify the rubric until they can
